text: The Buddhist teaching of anatta — non-self — is often misread as the claim that the self does not exist. More precisely, it denies the existence of a permanent, unchanging, independently-arising self. What we call "I" is a process: a continuous stream of sensation, perception, mental formation, and consciousness that Buddhist analysis divides into the five aggregates, or skandhas. Thich Nhat Hanh reframes this not as loss but as liberation: when the separate self dissolves, what remains is participation in the web of interbeing. The fear that losing selfhood means losing everything inverts the actual experience practitioners report — not diminishment, but expansion.
question: According to the passage, what does the Buddhist teaching of anatta actually deny?

options.text: That consciousness survives the death of the body
That human beings have any form of continuous experience
That individual perception has any validity as a guide to reality
That a permanent, unchanging, independently-arising self exists
